Company-facing proposal

Client Visualization & Private Commission Experience

A proposal to help One Off transform technical drawings into premium client approval journeys, private project pages, progress storytelling, and marketing-ready visual assets.

Prepared by Lesly Yankauskas Client Visualization & Experience Director · Bespoke furniture, architectural metalwork, luxury interiors, visual approval systems.
Thesis Pilot Portal Workflow Comments Pricing Questions
Executive thesis

This is not a 2D-to-3D task. It is a client confidence system.

A single render can show an object. A client visualization system helps a VIP client understand the piece, approve decisions, feel connected to the process, and experience the commission as something rare and cared for.

For high-value custom work, the client is not only buying metal, wood, glass, and craft. They are buying confidence, anticipation, status, and trust.

$50k–$100k+Possible value range for a bespoke architectural furniture commission. Final pricing varies by scope.
1 pageA private client page can hold the mood, visuals, progress, decisions, and comments in one place.
4 phasesConcept, approval, fabrication updates, final reveal.
1 systemReusable across future commissions, not rebuilt from scratch each time.
Respect for craft Client reassurance Premium approvals Marketing reuse
What I understand about One Off

Your work deserves a presentation layer equal to the object itself.

One Off’s value is not commodity furniture. It is custom work, material judgment, designer collaboration, fabrication intelligence, and pieces that have to feel right inside a specific environment.

The current request

“Can you make a 3D rendering from this 2D drawing?”

The deeper business opportunity

“Can we give VIP clients a more confident, beautiful, organized, and emotionally connected way to approve expensive custom commissions?”

My intention

I am not trying to replace your craft, CAD process, or fabrication knowledge. I want to protect it — by giving clients a clearer way to see, approve, and value the work before it reaches the shop floor.

Pilot case scenario

Bar Window Cabinet Commission

A VIP client orders a custom bar-window cabinet. The client should not only receive a technical drawing or one render. They should receive a guided commission experience.

Original technical elevation: fabrication logic, not client emotion.
CAD / shop view: exact structure, window logic, hardware notes.
Client visual: material, scale, status, and environment.
Context preview: useful for presentation, review, and storytelling.
Approval visual exampleSame design language, richer client understanding: scale, mood, finish, interior context, and perceived value.

What is already known from the request

  • The piece is a large cabinet / bar-window assembly.
  • The four tall windows are specified as 3/4 × 1 1/2 tube.
  • The lattice infill is not finalized and can be omitted for now.
  • The client wants to see the object in 3D because 2D is not enough for presentation.
  • The opportunity is to show the piece as a luxury object, not merely as geometry.
New business layer

Private Commission Portal

For a high-value client, every piece should feel like a gem. A private page gives the client a living connection to the commission: concept, approvals, progress, and final reveal.

Private client page · example

The Ranch Bar Window Cabinet

Current phaseApproval visuals
1
Design directionMood, material tone, placement, and client intent are documented before deep detailing.
2
Visual approvalFront view, 45° view, material options, detail notes, and movement explanation.
3
Fabrication progressShop updates, finish samples, milestone notes, and controlled client communication.
4
Final revealInstallation photo, short video, project story, and marketing-ready archive.

Why this helps the client

The client feels informed instead of anxious. They can see the direction, comment clearly, approve decisions, and feel the object becoming real.

Why this helps One Off

The team spends less time explaining scattered screenshots and more time building. Decisions are documented. Approval is clearer. The project becomes easier to communicate and easier to sell.

Video layer

Three simple videos can make the commission feel alive.

These can be simple, controlled product videos generated from approved visuals: a left-right inspection, a material/detail study, and a client approval summary. The goal is not entertainment. The goal is confidence.

▶

Video 01 · Client inspection

Slow left-to-right camera move around the cabinet. No redesign, no movement of the furniture.

Use for: first emotional buy-in and “this is what you are receiving” confidence.

▶

Video 02 · Materials & details

Close inspection of wood, blackened metal frames, glass, stone, and hardware.

Use for: premium detail review, finish discussion, and designer/client alignment.

▶

Video 03 · Final approval

Clean approval pass with final angles, final mood, and decision points.

Use for: final confirmation before deeper detailing or fabrication handoff.

Workflow analysis

From scattered communication to a luxury approval system.

⚠ Basic render approach

  • One isolated 3D image.
  • Client sees the object but not the journey.
  • Questions stay scattered across text, email, and screenshots.
  • Visuals may not clarify approval decisions.
  • Asset is used once and then disappears.

✓ Director-level system

  • Private client page per commission.
  • Concept, approval, progress, and final reveal in one place.
  • Client comments are pinned to the exact visual area.
  • One Off receives clearer approvals and fewer misunderstandings.
  • Assets convert into future sales and marketing material.
Phase 01 · Client intent

Understand the commission

Space, mood, material expectation, designer intent, budget sensitivity, decision makers, and where the client may feel uncertainty.

Phase 02 · Fast visual direction

Show direction early

Create quick visual mockups and mood references before every detail is modeled. This avoids wasting high-fidelity work on the wrong emotional direction.

Phase 03 · Approval package

Make the client confident

Front view, 45° view, detail closeups, simple video, finish options, and clear notes on what is confirmed versus conceptual.

Phase 04 · Technical fidelity

Align with fabrication reality

Update visuals from refined drawings, dimensions, materials, hardware logic, and installation conditions.

Phase 05 · Progress updates

Keep the client connected

Post shop photos, finish samples, milestone updates, and small progress notes to the private commission page.

Phase 06 · Marketing archive

Turn the project into brand equity

Convert final visuals, videos, and story into a case study, Instagram assets, and future client presentation material.

Interactive approval concept

Client comments should attach to the exact visual detail.

In the full software version, a VIP client could tap a detail on the image, leave a comment, and send that comment directly to One Off’s team, designer, or project manager.

Tap anywhere on the cabinet to create a client comment pin.

Production version

With your software agency, this becomes a real portal: comments saved to a database, email/slack notifications, status changes, approval logs, file uploads, and a clean record of client decisions.

Technology stack

Use technology only where it improves the client experience.

Client-facing layer

Private mobile project page, annotated visuals, video updates, approval status, comments, notifications, and final reveal archive.

HTML/CSS/JSPrivate linksMobile-first UX

Visualization layer

Concept visuals, refined product renders, 45° views, detail closeups, material studies, and short product videos.

3D modelingRenderingVideo generationCompositing

Future interactive layer

Web-based 3D viewers, AR previews, client-selectable finish options, designer approvals, and project-management integrations.

glTF/GLBThree.jsUSDZ/ARCRM/API
Recommendation & pay plan

Start with one paid pilot. Build the system only after value is proven.

The right first move is not a giant software build. The right first move is one premium commission pilot that demonstrates better approval, clearer communication, and stronger client experience.

Option A

Strategic Visualization Pilot

$2.5k–$7.5k

One project, clear scope, fast proof of value.

  • Review drawings and client-facing risks.
  • 1–2 concept visuals.
  • Hero render + 45° view.
  • Short strategy note for approval workflow.

Best for testing the relationship without overbuilding.

Option B

Private Commission Portal

$5k–$12k

A private client page for one high-value commission.

  • Mobile client page with visuals and status.
  • 3 video placeholders or embedded videos.
  • Approval notes and decision log.
  • Client questionnaire and progress sections.

Best for VIP clients and designer collaboration.

Option C

Premium Commission Experience

$12k–$25k+

Full visual journey for a major bespoke project.

  • Portal + refined visuals + material studies.
  • Short cinematic videos.
  • Progress update system.
  • Final reveal and marketing archive.

Best for $50k–$100k+ custom commissions where experience supports perceived value.

Option D

Fractional Director Retainer

$6k–$15k+/mo

Ongoing client visualization leadership across projects.

  • Visual intake for new commissions.
  • Presentation standards and asset library.
  • Approval visuals and client portal oversight.
  • Reusable marketing/storytelling workflow.

Best when One Off wants a repeatable visual experience system, not one-off rendering help.

Recommended 30-day plan

Practical implementation without disrupting the shop.

Week 1

Scope & intake

Collect drawings, project context, decision makers, deadline, visual examples, and client concerns.

Week 2

Concept and approval visuals

Create a small set of client-facing visuals and identify what needs technical confirmation.

Week 3

Private portal prototype

Build one mobile client page with status, visuals, videos, notes, and comment/approval flow.

Week 4

Review and system decision

Evaluate whether this reduces confusion, improves client experience, and should become a repeatable service layer.

Questionnaire

Questions before scoping the pilot

1. Client valueWhat is the approximate value range of commissions where client visualization would materially improve confidence or approval?
2. Decision makersWho approves the project: homeowner, designer, architect, contractor, internal owner, or multiple people?
3. Current frictionWhere do clients usually get confused: scale, material, finish, movement, installation, cost, or timing?
4. Existing workflowWhat files exist at each stage: sketch, PDF, DWG/DXF, CAD screenshots, shop drawings, photos, finish samples?
5. Portal usefulnessWould clients benefit from one private project page with visuals, status, approvals, comments, and progress updates?
6. CommunicationWhere should client comments go in a future system: email, shared inbox, CRM, project management tool, or internal dashboard?
7. MarketingWhich completed commissions could become case studies using “drawing → visualization → fabrication → final piece” storytelling?
8. First pilotIs the bar-window cabinet the right first project to test this client visualization experience?
Closing recommendation

One project can become the prototype for a better client experience.

I would start with the bar-window cabinet as a contained pilot: not just a render, but a premium approval experience. If it helps the client understand faster, approve with more confidence, and feel more connected, the workflow can become a repeatable offering for future One Off commissions.
